name: Unnamed Area (Agatized Coral #2)
short_description: Hunt for agatized coral and fossilized shells in this flat Florida terrain near Tampa. A productive spot for collectors seeking colorful agate specimens and petrified marine material.
description:
This unnamed rockhounding site in Hillsborough County offers access to agatized coral and chalcedony specimens typical of Florida's Cenozoic marine deposits. The flat terrain makes this location accessible for rockhounds of varying skill levels who are interested in Florida's rich fossil and agate heritage. The area's gentle slopes and lack of significant elevation change create an ideal environment for systematic searching without demanding physical exertion. Collectors typically find colorful agate nodules, chalcedony fragments, and occasional fossilized coral pieces scattered across the property. The location attracts both beginners and experienced rockhounders seeking authentic Florida specimens without the commercial fee-dig experience.

The agatized material here formed during Florida's extended periods as a shallow marine environment, when silica-rich groundwater percolated through carbonate sediments and gradually replaced coral structures and shells with microcrystalline quartz. This process, occurring over millions of years during the Miocene and Pliocene epochs, created the distinctive banded and colorful agate specimens found at this site today. The relatively recent geological exposure of these deposits through erosion and land clearing has made them accessible to surface collectors.

Rockhounders should expect to find small to medium-sized agate pieces, often exhibiting bands of white, tan, orange, and rust coloring. The material is durable and takes a polish well, making these finds popular for cabochon cutting and decorative display. Patience and careful scanning of exposed soil and debris are key to success at this location.
history: This area represents part of Florida's extensive Cenozoic marine heritage, where warm shallow seas during the Miocene and Pliocene epochs allowed prolific coral growth and shell accumulation. Silica-rich groundwater gradually agatized these carbonate structures, creating the colorful agate and chalcedony deposits that rockhounders find today in the Tampa Bay region.
tips: Search disturbed soil areas, old eroded banks, and spaces around the periphery where material is most exposed. Use a small hand rake or brush to clear light vegetation and examine soil surfaces for banded agate fragments. Look for specimens with distinct color banding - whites, tans, and orange-rust hues indicate well-agatized pieces worth keeping. The best finds often appear after rain when freshly exposed material becomes visible.
terrain_notes: Flat terrain typical of coastal Florida with minimal elevation change. Ground consists of sandy soil, clay, and exposed beds of fossiliferous sediment. Vegetation includes typical Florida scrub, palmettos, and low brush. Walking distance from parking area varies depending on access point, but most productive areas are within 100-300 yards. Footing is generally stable on sandy/clay ground, though areas can be muddy after rainfall.
best_season: October - April. Florida's cooler and drier months make outdoor rockhounding comfortable. Summer heat and afternoon thunderstorms create hazardous conditions and reduce ground visibility.
written_directions: From downtown Tampa, take I-75 southbound approximately 8 miles toward St. Petersburg. Exit at Fletcher Avenue (exit 264) and head east for about 2 miles. The site is located in the Hillsborough County area near the coordinates provided - use GPS navigation with the latitude/longitude 27.886168, -82.480831 to reach the general vicinity. Local knowledge or prior contact with rockhounding groups familiar with this unnamed site is recommended for precise parking and access.
road_conditions: Paved roads to general area; final approach may be unpaved or through private property roads
parking_notes: Limited informal parking; rockhounders typically park along roadsides or in small pullouts. No designated parking lot. Capacity varies by specific access point.
collection_rules: This is private land - permission from property owners is required before collecting. Personal collection only where permitted. Respect all posted no-trespassing signs and private property boundaries.
rules:
Obtain permission from property owner before accessing
Personal collection only
Respect private property boundaries
No commercial collection
Restore any disturbed areas
nearest_services: Tampa, approximately 10-12 miles north. Full services including gas, restaurants, hospitals, and shopping available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most visitors. No significant hills or obstacles. Unpaved final approach may present minor challenges for wheelchairs or walkers. Sturdy shoes recommended for muddy areas after rain. Generally suitable for families and casual rockhounders.

faq.question: What types of agate can I find at this unnamed coral site near Tampa?
faq.answer: This location yields agatized coral and chalcedony specimens with distinctive banding in white, tan, orange, and rust colors. The agatized pieces form from coral and shell structures that were gradually replaced by silica over millions of years during Florida's marine periods. Most specimens are small to medium-sized and take a nice polish for lapidary work.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at this Hillsborough County site?
faq.answer: This is private property, so you do not need a permit but you must obtain permission from the property owner before collecting. Always check for posted signs and contact local rockhounding groups familiar with this site to confirm current access and ownership status.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to collect agatized coral near Tampa?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Florida's cooler and drier months provide comfortable collecting conditions and better ground visibility. Summer heat, humidity, and afternoon thunderstorms make rockhounding more difficult and potentially hazardous.

faq.question: Is this rockhounding site beginner-friendly and suitable for families?
faq.answer: Yes. The flat terrain, minimal hiking required, and accessible ground conditions make this site ideal for beginners and families with children ages 6 and up. No special equipment or expertise is needed - just a rake, brush, and patience to scan soil surfaces for banded agate fragments.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need to collect at this unnamed agate site?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand rake or brush to clear vegetation and expose material, a collecting bag for specimens, sturdy walking shoes, sunscreen, and plenty of water. A magnifying glass helps identify quality pieces with good banding. After rain, muddy areas may benefit from a change of footwear.

faq.question: How far is this agatized coral location from Tampa and what are road conditions?
faq.answer: The site is approximately 10-12 miles south of Tampa, accessible via I-75 and Fletcher Avenue. Paved roads lead to the general area, though final approach roads may be unpaved. Standard passenger vehicles can typically reach the location, though the specific parking area depends on current property access arrangements.

meta_title: Agatized Coral Rockhounding Near Tampa, Florida
meta_description: Find agate, chalcedony, and fossilized coral at this private rockhounding site near Tampa, FL. Easy flat terrain, beginner-friendly, October-April access.
